text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide adhesions between the colon and the abdominal wall. Subsequently, the bladder is dissected posteriorly, releasing it from the anterior abdominal wall to access the retropubic space, or Retzius space. The bladder is mobilized for a tension-free closure.

The surgeon performs a cystostomy on the bladder dome, using an ultrasonic scalpel to dissect the suspicious area with safety margins. The previously localized area through cystoscopy is visible upon opening the bladder, with a gross margin of approximately one centimeter maintained to ensure adequate reservoir capacity.

The surgeon performs vesicle closure using a running suture technique in two separate planes of mucosa and musculature, with a Foley catheter visible in the bladder. The closure is done intracorporeally using a 2-0 vicryl suture.
The surgeon closes the second plane of the bladder wall with a running suture using V-lock, tying the two threads of the suture together at the end.

The surgeon performs a leak check using saline solution, followed by mobilization of the sigmoid colon through lysing of omental adhesions to the abdominal wall. A sigmoidectomy is performed while preserving the left colic artery. The proximal rectum is divided using an endogea stapler, and tissue perfusion is assessed with endocyanin green to identify the optimal site for bowel division.

The surgeon performs an end-to-end circular anastomosis in the pelvis, leaving a low-pressure suction drain in place. A routine anastomotic air leak test is conducted. The clip also shows a normal cystoscopy three weeks post-operation.
